A: Gap junctions provide channels that allow ions to flow freely between cells, enabling action potentials or ion flow to travel across the entire muscle mass simultaneously.

Q: Why is unitary smooth muscle also called "visceral" smooth muscle?
A: It is named so because it is primarily located in the walls of internal organs (viscera).

Q: What are some specific locations where unitary smooth muscle is found in the body?
A: - The gastrointestinal tract
- Bile ducts
- Ureters
- The uterus
- Numerous blood vessels

Q: How do multi-unit and unitary smooth muscles differ regarding the presence of action potentials?
A: - Unitary smooth muscle typically generates action potentials (spike potentials or plateaus).
- Multi-unit smooth muscle usually does not develop action potentials because the fibers are too small.

Q: What is a junctional potential in the context of multi-unit smooth muscle?
A: It is a local depolarization caused by a neurotransmitter that spreads across the small fiber to trigger contraction without a full action potential.

Q: How does the physical arrangement of contractile filaments differ between smooth muscle and skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle lacks the highly organized, striated pattern of actin and myosin found in skeletal muscle; instead, it uses dense bodies as anchors.

Q: What is the function of dense bodies in smooth muscle?
A: Dense bodies serve the same purpose as Z disks in skeletal muscle, acting as attachment points for actin filaments either within the cell or on the cell membrane.

Q: How are adjacent cells in smooth muscle bonded together to transmit force?
A: Some dense bodies located on the cell membranes of neighboring cells are linked by protein bridges.

Q: What is the ratio of actin to myosin filaments in smooth muscle compared to skeletal muscle?
A: In smooth muscle, there are typically 5 to 10 times as many actin filaments as there are myosin filaments.

Q: Describe the "side polar" cross-bridge arrangement in smooth muscle myosin.
A: The bridges on one side of the filament hinge in one direction, while those on the opposite side hinge the other way, allowing the myosin to pull actin filaments in opposite directions simultaneously.

Q: What is the functional advantage of the side polar cross-bridge arrangement in smooth muscle?
A: It allows smooth muscle cells to contract much more extensively, up to 80% of their total length, compared to the less than 30% limitation in skeletal muscle.

Q: How does the cycling frequency of myosin cross-bridges compare between smooth and skeletal muscle?
A: Cross-bridge cycling in smooth muscle is significantly slower, occurring at a rate only 1/10 to 1/300 that of skeletal muscle.

Q: Why is the energy requirement for smooth muscle contraction so much lower than for skeletal muscle?
A: Because of the slow attachment and detachment cycles of the cross-bridges, smooth muscle requires only 1/10 to 1/300 as much energy to maintain the same level of tension.

Q: What is the typical duration of a single smooth muscle contraction?
A: While it varies by type, a typical total contraction time is 1 to 3 seconds, which is roughly 30 times longer than the average skeletal muscle twitch.

Q: What is the "latch mechanism" in smooth muscle?
A: It is a state where smooth muscle maintains a full force of contraction for long periods with minimal continued excitation and very little energy consumption.

Q: Define the "stress-relaxation" property of unitary smooth muscle.
A: It is the ability of a hollow organ to return to its original pressure level shortly after its volume has been significantly increased or decreased.

Q: What regulatory protein replaces troponin in smooth muscle contraction?
A: Calmodulin.

Q: From where does smooth muscle primarily obtain the calcium ions needed for contraction?
A: Unlike skeletal muscle which relies on the sarcoplasmic reticulum, smooth muscle obtains most of its calcium from the extracellular fluid through calcium channels.

Q: What is the "latent period" in smooth muscle contraction?
A: It is the time required for calcium to diffuse into the cell from the extracellular fluid before contraction starts, averaging 200 to 300 milliseconds.

Q: What are caveolae in smooth muscle cells?
A: They are small indentations in the cell membrane that act as a rudimentary version of the transverse tubule system found in skeletal muscle.

Q: What determines the cessation of contraction in smooth muscle?
A: Contraction stops when calcium levels fall and the enzyme myosin phosphatase removes the phosphate group from the myosin head.

Q: How do the neuromuscular junctions in smooth muscle differ from those in skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle lacks the highly structured motor end plates of skeletal muscle; instead, autonomic nerves use "diffuse junctions" or "varicosities" to release neurotransmitters.

Q: What are varicosities in autonomic nerve fibers?
A: They are multiple enlarged areas along terminal axons where neurotransmitters are stored and secreted through the gaps in the surrounding Schwann cells.

Q: What are "contact junctions" in smooth muscle?
A: Found primarily in multi-unit smooth muscle, these are junctions where varicosities are separated from the muscle membrane by only 20 to 30 nanometers, allowing for faster contraction.

Q: Which two neurotransmitters are most commonly secreted by autonomic nerves to smooth muscle?
A: Acetylcholine and norepinephrine.

Q: What determines whether a neurotransmitter will excite or inhibit a specific smooth muscle?
A: The type of receptor protein present on the smooth muscle cell membrane (either an excitatory or inhibitory receptor).

Q: What is the typical resting membrane potential of smooth muscle?
A: Usually about -50 to -60 millivolts.

Q: What are "slow wave potentials" in unitary smooth muscle?
A: They are rhythmic local changes in membrane potential that, if they reach a threshold of about -35 millivolts, can spontaneously trigger action potentials.

Q: What are pacemaker waves?
A: They are slow waves that reach threshold and initiate repetitive sequences of action potentials, leading to rhythmic contractions in smooth muscle masses like the gut.

Q: How does unitary smooth muscle react to being stretched?
A: Excessive stretch often causes the muscle to generate spontaneous action potentials, leading to rhythmic contractions that help move contents through hollow organs.

Q: List local tissue factors that can cause smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation.
A: - Lack of oxygen
- Excess carbon dioxide
- Increased hydrogen ion concentration (acidity)
- Adenosine
- Lactic acid
- Increased potassium ions
- Nitric oxide
- Increased temperature

Q: How can hormones inhibit smooth muscle contraction?
A: By closing sodium or calcium channels, or by opening potassium channels to cause hyperpolarization (increased internal negativity).

Q: What second messengers can inhibit smooth muscle contraction?
A: Cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) and cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP).

topics.topic: Describe mechanism of smooth muscle contraction in comparison to skeletal muscle.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: How do the physical dimensions of smooth muscle fibers compare to those of skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle fibers are significantly smaller, typically measuring 1 to 5 micrometers in diameter and 20 to 500 micrometers in length, whereas skeletal muscle fibers are roughly 30 times wider and hundreds of times longer.

Q: What are the two primary classifications of smooth muscle?
A: Smooth muscle is generally categorized into multi-unit smooth muscle and unitary (or single-unit) smooth muscle.

Q: What characterizes multi-unit smooth muscle fibers?
A: These consist of distinct, individual fibers that function independently, are usually controlled by a single nerve ending, and are insulated from one another by a basement membrane-like layer of collagen and glycoprotein.

Q: What are some examples of multi-unit smooth muscle in the human body?
A: Examples include the ciliary and iris muscles of the eye and the piloerector muscles responsible for hair erection.

Q: How does unitary smooth muscle function as a single unit?
A: Thousands of fibers are bundled or layered together with cell membranes adhered at multiple points, allowing force transmission. They are connected by gap junctions that permit ions and action potentials to flow freely between cells.

Q: Where is unitary smooth muscle typically located?
A: It is found in the walls of various viscera, such as the uterus, bile ducts, gastrointestinal tract, ureters, and many blood vessels.

Q: Which contractile proteins are common to both smooth and skeletal muscle?
A: Both muscle types utilize actin and myosin filaments to generate attractive forces for contraction.

Q: What regulatory protein complex is present in skeletal muscle but absent in smooth muscle?
A: The troponin complex.

Q: What serves as the primary trigger for the contractile process in both smooth and skeletal muscle?
A: An increase in calcium ions.

Q: What is the structural equivalent of skeletal muscle Z disks in smooth muscle?
A: Dense bodies.

Q: How are actin filaments organized within a smooth muscle cell?
A: Large numbers of actin filaments are attached to dense bodies, some of which are fixed to the cell membrane while others float within the cytoplasm.

Q: What is the ratio of actin to myosin filaments in smooth muscle?
A: There are typically 5 to 10 times as many actin filaments as there are myosin filaments.

Q: What is a "side polar" cross-bridge arrangement in smooth muscle?
A: It is a configuration where the myosin heads on one side hinge in one direction while those on the opposite side hinge the other way, allowing the filament to pull actin in opposite directions simultaneously.

Q: How does the contraction range of smooth muscle compare to skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle can contract up to 80% of its length, whereas skeletal muscle is generally limited to less than 30%.

Q: How does the cycling frequency of myosin cross-bridges differ in smooth muscle?
A: The attachment, release, and reattachment of cross-bridges is much slower in smooth muscle, occurring at a frequency of 1/10 to 1/300 of that seen in skeletal muscle.

Q: Why is the cross-bridge cycling slower in smooth muscle?
A: The cross-bridge heads in smooth muscle possess significantly lower ATPase activity, which slows the degradation of ATP used for movement.

Q: How does the energy requirement for maintaining tension compare between smooth and skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle requires only 1/10 to 1/300 as much energy to maintain the same level of tension as skeletal muscle.

Q: What is the typical duration of a smooth muscle contraction cycle?
A: Total contraction time is usually 1 to 3 seconds, which is roughly 30 times longer than the average skeletal muscle fiber contraction.

Q: Despite fewer myosin filaments, how does the maximum force of smooth muscle compare to skeletal muscle?
A: The maximum force is often greater in smooth muscle (4 to 6 kg/cm2) than in skeletal muscle (3 to 4 kg/cm2) because cross-bridges remain attached to actin for longer periods.

Q: What is the "latch mechanism" in smooth muscle?
A: It is a state where the muscle maintains full contractile force with very little energy consumption and minimal continued excitatory signaling.

Q: What are stress-relaxation and reverse stress-relaxation?
A: These are abilities of smooth muscle to return to nearly its original pressure level within seconds or minutes after a sudden change in volume (stretching or shortening).

Q: What regulatory protein does smooth muscle use in place of troponin?
A: Calmodulin.

Q: What is the first step in the chemical sequence of smooth muscle contraction?
A: Intracellular calcium concentration increases due to entry from extracellular fluid or release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Q: What happens after calcium binds to calmodulin in smooth muscle?
A: The calcium-calmodulin complex joins with and activates an enzyme called myosin light chain kinase (MLCK).

Q: What is the role of myosin light chain kinase (MLCK)?
A: It phosphorylates the regulatory light chain on the myosin head.

Q: What must occur to the myosin head in smooth muscle before it can bind to actin?
A: Its regulatory light chain must be phosphorylated by MLCK.

Q: Where does the majority of calcium used for smooth muscle contraction originate?
A: Most of the calcium enters the cell from the extracellular fluid via calcium channels.

Q: How developed is the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) in smooth muscle compared to skeletal muscle?
A: The SR is only slightly developed in most smooth muscle, whereas it is the primary calcium source for skeletal muscle.

Q: What are caveolae in smooth muscle?
A: They are small cell membrane invaginations that abut the sarcoplasmic reticulum and are believed to function similarly to the transverse (T) tubule system in skeletal muscle.

Q: What is the latent period in smooth muscle contraction?
A: It is the 200 to 300 millisecond delay required for calcium to diffuse into the cell after a stimulus, which is about 50 times longer than in skeletal muscle.

Q: How does extracellular calcium concentration affect smooth muscle compared to skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle is highly dependent on extracellular calcium levels, and contraction may cease if these levels drop significantly; skeletal muscle force is relatively unaffected by these changes.

Q: What enzyme is responsible for stopping smooth muscle contraction?
A: Myosin phosphatase.

Q: What are the two requirements for smooth muscle relaxation?
A: - Calcium must be pumped out of the cell or into the SR.
- Myosin phosphatase must remove the phosphate group from the myosin regulatory light chain.

Q: How does the resting membrane potential of smooth muscle compare to skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle has a resting potential of about -50 to -60 millivolts, which is approximately 30 millivolts less negative than that of skeletal muscle.

Q: What are the two forms of action potentials seen in visceral smooth muscle?
A: Spike potentials and action potentials with plateaus.

Q: Which ion is primarily responsible for the action potential in smooth muscle?
A: Calcium ions (flowing through voltage-gated calcium channels), unlike skeletal muscle which relies more on sodium.

Q: What are slow wave potentials in smooth muscle?
A: These are self-excitatory, rhythmic fluctuations in membrane potential that can act as "pacemaker waves" to trigger action potentials if they reach a threshold of about -35 millivolts.

Q: How can physical stretch induce smooth muscle contraction?
A: Sufficient stretching generates spontaneous action potentials through a combination of slow wave potentials and a decrease in membrane negativity.

Q: How do neuromuscular junctions in smooth muscle differ from the motor end plates of skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle utilizes "diffuse junctions" where autonomic nerve fibers secrete neurotransmitters from multiple bulbous enlargements called varicosities into the surrounding matrix.

Q: What are "contact junctions" in smooth muscle?
A: These are specialized varicosities separated from the muscle cell by only 20 to 30 nanometers, allowing for faster contraction (found mainly in multi-unit smooth muscle).

Q: Which neurotransmitters are most important for smooth muscle control?
A: Acetylcholine and norepinephrine.

Q: Can smooth muscle contract without an action potential?
A: Yes, roughly half of smooth muscle contraction is initiated by non-electrical factors like local tissue chemicals or hormones.

Q: What local chemical factors can induce smooth muscle relaxation (vasodilation) in blood vessels?
A: Lack of oxygen, excess carbon dioxide, increased hydrogen ions (acidity), adenosine, lactic acid, and increased body temperature.

Q: How can hormones inhibit smooth muscle contraction?
A: By closing sodium/calcium channels, opening potassium channels (causing hyperpolarization), or activating secondary messengers like cAMP or cGMP to reduce intracellular calcium.

topics.topic: Explain the physiological anatomy of neuromuscular junction of smooth muscle
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: How does the structural organization of the smooth muscle neuromuscular junction compare to that of skeletal muscle?
A: The smooth muscle version is far less structured and lacks the highly organized motor end plate found in skeletal muscle.

Q: In what manner do autonomic nerve fibers typically distribute themselves over smooth muscle?
A: They generally branch out in a diffuse pattern across a layer of muscle cells.

Q: Do autonomic nerve fibers usually make direct contact with smooth muscle cell membranes?
A: No, they typically form "diffuse junctions" where they stay a short distance away from the muscle cells.

Q: Where are neurotransmitters released in a diffuse junction of smooth muscle?
A: They are secreted into the matrix coating that surrounds the smooth muscle cells.

Q: What is the typical distance between a nerve terminal and a smooth muscle cell in a diffuse junction?
A: The distance ranges from a few nanometers to several micrometers.

Q: How is a signal transmitted to the inner layers of a smooth muscle mass if only the surface layer is innervated?
- Through the conduction of action potentials directly through the muscle mass.
- By the further diffusion of neurotransmitter substances.

Q: What are the multiple swellings found along the axes of fine terminal axons in smooth muscle called?
A: These swellings are known as varicosities.

Q: What is the status of the Schwann cell covering at the site of an axonal varicosity?
A: The Schwann cell is interrupted at that specific point to permit the release of transmitter substances.

Q: What is contained within the varicosities of autonomic nerve fibers?
A: They contain vesicles filled with neurotransmitter substances.

Q: Which two neurotransmitters are most frequently found in the vesicles of smooth muscle nerve endings?
A: Acetylcholine and norepinephrine.

Q: What specific name is given to smooth muscle junctions where varicosities are separated from the muscle membrane by only 20 to 30 nanometers?
A: They are called contact junctions.

Q: How does the width of the synaptic cleft in a smooth muscle contact junction compare to a skeletal muscle junction?
A: They are roughly the same width, measuring about 20 to 30 nanometers.

Q: How does the functional speed of a contact junction compare to a diffuse junction in smooth muscle?
A: Contraction occurs significantly faster in fibers stimulated by contact junctions than in those stimulated by diffuse junctions.

Q: What replaces the "branching end feet" of skeletal motor end plates in smooth muscle innervation?
A: Diffuse varicosities located along the terminal axons.

Q: In a diffuse junction, what substance must the transmitter pass through before reaching the muscle cell?
A: The matrix coating of the smooth muscle.

topics.topic: Explain the excitatory and inhibitory transmitters secreted at Neuro Muscular Junction (NMJ) of smooth muscles.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: Which two chemical substances are identified as the most significant neurotransmitters released by autonomic nerves at smooth muscle neuromuscular junctions?
A: 
- Acetylcholine
- Norepinephrine

Q: Does a specific neurotransmitter, such as acetylcholine, always produce the same effect on all smooth muscle tissues?
A: No. A neurotransmitter like acetylcholine can act as an excitatory agent in some organs while serving as an inhibitory agent in others.

Q: What is the typical relationship between the effects of acetylcholine and norepinephrine on a single smooth muscle fiber?
A: They usually have opposing effects; if acetylcholine excites a specific fiber, norepinephrine typically inhibits it, and if acetylcholine inhibits a fiber, norepinephrine typically excites it.

Q: What determines whether a neurotransmitter like norepinephrine will excite or inhibit a smooth muscle cell?
A: The specific type of receptor protein located on the surface of the muscle cell membrane (either an excitatory or inhibitory receptor).

Q: How does norepinephrine affect the smooth muscle located in the walls of the intestines?
A: It acts as an inhibitory substance, preventing contraction.

Q: What is the effect of norepinephrine on the smooth muscle found within blood vessels?
A: It acts as an excitatory substance, stimulating contraction.

Q: Through what electrical mechanism do excitatory hormones or local factors typically initiate smooth muscle contraction?
A: They open sodium or calcium ion channels, which causes the cell membrane to depolarize.

Q: What are the two primary ways that inhibitory factors can change ion channel activity to prevent smooth muscle contraction?
A: 
- By closing sodium or calcium channels to stop the entry of positive ions. 
- By opening potassium channels to allow positive ions to exit the cell.

Q: What is the name for the state of increased internal negativity in a smooth muscle cell that leads to the inhibition of contraction?
A: Hyperpolarization.

Q: Can hormones stimulate smooth muscle contraction without altering the membrane potential?
A: Yes. Some hormones can trigger contraction by causing the internal release of calcium ions from the sarcoplasmic reticulum without changing the electrical potential of the membrane.

Q: Which intracellular "second messengers" may be activated by certain receptors to facilitate smooth muscle inhibition?
A: 
- Cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP)
- Cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP)

Q: How do second messengers like cAMP and cGMP facilitate the relaxation of smooth muscle?
A: They stimulate pumps that move calcium ions out of the sarcoplasm, either by sending them out of the cell or into the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Q: What are the multiple bulges along autonomic terminal axons called that secrete neurotransmitters at smooth muscle junctions?
A: Varicosities.

Q: How does the delivery of neurotransmitters in smooth muscle "diffuse junctions" differ from skeletal muscle junctions?
A: In smooth muscle, neurotransmitters are secreted into the surrounding matrix and must diffuse to the muscle cells, rather than being released directly onto a structured end plate.

Q: In multi-unit smooth muscle, what is the name of the local electrical change caused by a neurotransmitter that triggers contraction without an action potential?
A: A junctional potential.

Q: What are some examples of local tissue chemical factors that can cause smooth muscle relaxation (vasodilation) in small blood vessels?
A: 
- Lack of oxygen
- Increased carbon dioxide levels
- Higher hydrogen ion concentration (acidity)
- Increased levels of adenosine, lactic acid, or potassium ions
- Presence of nitric oxide
- Increased body temperature

topics.topic: Explain the depolarization of multiunit smooth muscles without action potentials.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: How do multiunit smooth muscle fibers typically respond to stimulation compared to unitary smooth muscle?
A: They function as separate, discrete fibers that contract independently, primarily governed by nerve impulses rather than non-nervous triggers.

Q: Which neurotransmitters are mainly responsible for inducing depolarization in multiunit smooth muscle?
A: Norepinephrine and acetylcholine.

Q: What prevents the development of typical action potentials in multiunit smooth muscle fibers?
A: The fibers are physically too small to produce a self-propagating action potential.

Q: What is the required condition for a self-propagating action potential to occur in visceral unitary smooth muscle?
A: At least 30 to 40 smooth muscle fibers must undergo depolarization at the same time.

Q: What is the specific name for the local depolarization created by a neurotransmitter in multiunit smooth muscle?
A: It is called a junctional potential.

Q: How does the electrical change spread across a multiunit smooth muscle fiber in the absence of an action potential?
A: The junctional potential moves across the fiber through electrotonic spread.

Q: Why is a junctional potential sufficient to cause contraction in multiunit smooth muscle?
A: Because the cells are so small, the electrotonic spread of the local depolarization can cover the entire fiber and trigger the contractile mechanism.

Q: What are three specific examples of multiunit smooth muscle in the human body?
A: - The piloerector muscles (associated with hair follicles)
- The iris muscle of the eye
- The ciliary muscle of the eye

Q: How is the innervation of multiunit smooth muscle fibers structured?
A: Each fiber acts independently and is often controlled by its own individual nerve ending.

Q: What substance surrounds the exterior of multiunit smooth muscle fibers to provide insulation?
A: A thin coating similar to a basement membrane, composed of a blend of fine collagen and glycoproteins.

Q: How does the mechanism of contraction in multiunit smooth muscle differ from skeletal muscle regarding nerve signals?
A: While both respond to nerve signals, multiunit smooth muscle uses local junctional potentials and electrotonic spread to contract rather than always requiring an action potential.

topics.topic: Explain the local tissue factors and hormones that can cause smooth muscle contraction without action potential.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: Approximately what percentage of smooth muscle contractions are estimated to be triggered by stimulatory factors rather than action potentials?
A: About 50% of contractions in smooth muscle are initiated by direct stimulatory factors.

Q: Which two categories of non-nervous factors can trigger smooth muscle contraction without the need for an action potential?
A: - Hormonal signals
- Local chemical conditions in the surrounding tissue

Q: Which specific types of small blood vessels have minimal or no nervous innervation yet are highly reactive to local chemical changes?
A: - Arterioles
- Meta-arterioles
- Precapillary sphincters

Q: How does a local deficiency of oxygen influence smooth muscle in small blood vessels?
A: It acts as a local tissue factor that induces muscle relaxation and vasodilation.

Q: What effect does an overabundance of carbon dioxide have on vascular smooth muscle?
A: It leads to relaxation of the muscle and subsequent vasodilation.

Q: How does the concentration of hydrogen ions affect local smooth muscle in the vasculature?
A: An increase in hydrogen ion concentration promotes muscle relaxation and vasodilation.

Q: List the local metabolic and chemical factors mentioned that result in the relaxation of smooth muscle in small vessels.
A: - Adenosine
- Lactic acid
- Increased levels of potassium ions
- Nitric oxide

Q: In what way does an increase in body temperature affect local vascular smooth muscle?
A: It causes the muscle to relax, leading to vasodilation.

Q: How does a drop in blood pressure impact the contraction state of small blood vessels?
A: It reduces the physical stretch on the vascular smooth muscle, causing the vessels to dilate.

Q: Name the specific hormones identified as significant contributors to smooth muscle contraction or inhibition.
A: - Norepinephrine
- Epinephrine
- Angiotensin II
- Endothelin
- Vasopressin
- Oxytocin
- Serotonin
- Histamine

Q: What determines whether a hormone will stimulate contraction or induce inhibition in a smooth muscle cell?
A: The presence of either excitatory or inhibitory hormone-gated receptors on the cell's membrane.

Q: How can a hormone cause smooth muscle contraction through depolarization without initiating an action potential?
A: By opening calcium or sodium ion channels, which allows positive ions to enter and depolarize the cell membrane.

Q: Apart from membrane depolarization, what internal mechanism can hormones use to trigger smooth muscle contraction?
A: Activating membrane receptors that cause the internal sarcoplasmic reticulum to release calcium ions.

Q: Which ion channel changes are associated with the inhibition of smooth muscle by hormones or local factors?
A: - Closing of sodium and calcium channels.
- Opening of potassium channels.

Q: What is the electrical result of a hormone opening potassium channels in a smooth muscle cell?
A: The cell undergoes hyperpolarization, which is an increase in internal negativity that strongly prevents contraction.

Q: Which internal "second messenger" molecules can be stimulated by hormone receptors to inhibit smooth muscle contraction?
A: - Cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP)
- Cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP)

Q: How do second messengers like cAMP and cGMP specifically work to relax smooth muscle?
A: They alter enzyme phosphorylation to activate pumps that move calcium out of the sarcoplasm, either into the sarcoplasmic reticulum or out of the cell entirely.

Q: How can the hormone norepinephrine have different effects on the smooth muscle of the gut compared to the blood vessels?
A: It inhibits contraction in the intestinal smooth muscle but stimulates contraction in vascular smooth muscle, depending on the receptors present in those locations.

topics.topic: Explain the regulation of smooth muscle contraction by calcium ions.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the primary intracellular signal that triggers the contraction of smooth muscle?
A: An increase in the concentration of calcium ions within the cell.

Q: Which regulatory protein is notably absent in smooth muscle that is present in skeletal muscle?
A: Troponin.

Q: What regulatory protein does smooth muscle use in place of troponin to initiate the contractile process?
A: Calmodulin.

Q: What is the first step in the chemical sequence of smooth muscle contraction once calcium enters the cytosol?
A: Calcium ions bind reversibly with the regulatory protein calmodulin.

Q: What complex is formed after calcium enters the smooth muscle cytoplasm?
A: The calmodulin-calcium complex.

Q: What is the function of the calmodulin-calcium complex in smooth muscle?
A: It joins with and activates an enzyme called myosin light chain kinase (MLCK).

Q: What specific action does myosin light chain kinase perform to facilitate contraction?
A: It acts as a phosphorylating enzyme that phosphorylates the regulatory light chain of the myosin head.

Q: What must happen to the regulatory light chain of the myosin head for contraction to proceed in smooth muscle?
A: It must be phosphorylated by activated myosin light chain kinase.

Q: How does the phosphorylation of the regulatory light chain affect the myosin head's behavior?
A: It gives the myosin head the capacity to bind repetitively with the actin filament and go through the attachment-detachment cycling process.

Q: What happens to the myosin head when the regulatory light chain is not phosphorylated?
A: The attachment-detachment cycling of the myosin head with the actin filament does not take place.

Q: From which two sources can smooth muscle obtain the calcium ions needed for contraction?
A: - Influx from the extracellular fluid through calcium channels.
- Release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Q: How does the development of the sarcoplasmic reticulum in smooth muscle compare to that of skeletal muscle?
A: It is only slightly developed in most smooth muscle fibers.

Q: What is the primary source of calcium ions for contraction in most smooth muscle types?
A: The extracellular fluid.

Q: Why is there a significant latent period before smooth muscle begins to contract?
A: Time is required for calcium ions to diffuse into the cell from the extracellular fluid.

Q: What is the typical duration of the latent period for smooth muscle contraction?
A: Between 200 and 300 milliseconds.

Q: How much longer is the latent period of smooth muscle compared to skeletal muscle?
A: It is approximately 50 times as long.

Q: What are caveolae in smooth muscle cells?
A: Small invaginations of the cell membrane that abut the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Q: What is the suspected function of caveolae in smooth muscle?
A: They are believed to be a rudimentary version of the transverse tubule system found in skeletal muscle.

Q: What is the relationship between the extent of a smooth muscle's sarcoplasmic reticulum and its contraction speed?
A: Fibers with a more extensive sarcoplasmic reticulum generally contract more rapidly.

Q: How sensitive is smooth muscle contraction to the concentration of extracellular calcium?
A: It is highly dependent; contraction usually stops if the extracellular calcium concentration drops to 1/3 or 1/10 of its normal level.

Q: What mechanism is required to begin the relaxation of smooth muscle?
A: A calcium pump must remove calcium ions from the intracellular fluid.

Q: To where do the calcium pumps move ions during smooth muscle relaxation?
A: Either back into the extracellular fluid or into the sarcoplasmic reticulum (if it exists).

Q: What are the characteristics of the smooth muscle calcium pump compared to the skeletal muscle pump?
A: It requires ATP and is relatively slow-acting.

Q: Which enzyme is responsible for stopping the cycling of myosin heads in smooth muscle?
A: Myosin phosphatase.

Q: Where is myosin phosphatase located within the smooth muscle cell?
A: It is located in the cytosol.

Q: How does myosin phosphatase stop muscle contraction?
A: It splits the phosphate group from the regulatory light chain of the myosin head.

Q: What determines the total time required for smooth muscle to relax?
A: The concentration of active myosin phosphatase within the cell.

Q: How does the latch mechanism affect energy consumption during prolonged contraction?
A: It allows smooth muscle to maintain full contraction force with very little continued excitation and minuscule energy use.

Q: According to the proposed latch mechanism, what happens as the activation of myosin kinase and myosin phosphatase decreases?
A: The cycling frequency decreases, but the myosin heads stay attached to the actin for a longer proportion of the cycle, maintaining tension without degrading much ATP.

Q: In unitary smooth muscle, which ions are primarily responsible for the generation of action potentials?
A: Calcium ions.

Q: How does the behavior of calcium channels in smooth muscle compare to sodium channels in other tissues?
A: Calcium channels open much more slowly and remain open for a significantly longer duration.

Q: What dual role does calcium play during the action potential of visceral smooth muscle?
A: It generates the action potential and acts directly on the contractile mechanism to trigger contraction.

Q: How can hormones or local tissue factors cause smooth muscle contraction without an action potential?
A: They may trigger the release of calcium ions from the internal sarcoplasmic reticulum or open membrane calcium channels.

Q: How do second messengers like cAMP or cGMP inhibit smooth muscle contraction?
A: They activate pumps that move calcium out of the cell or into the sarcoplasmic reticulum, reducing the cytosolic calcium concentration.

topics.topic: Explain membrane potential and action potentials in smooth muscles.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What are the typical physical dimensions of a smooth muscle fiber?
A: They are usually 1 to 5 micrometers in diameter and 20 to 500 micrometers in length.

Q: How does the resting membrane potential of smooth muscle compare to that of skeletal muscle?
A: The intracellular potential of smooth muscle is roughly -50 to -60 millivolts, which is approximately 30 millivolts less negative than the resting potential of skeletal muscle.

Q: In which specific type of smooth muscle do action potentials typically occur?
A: Unitary smooth muscle, also known as visceral or syncytial smooth muscle.

Q: What are the two primary forms of action potentials observed in visceral smooth muscle?
A:
- Spike potentials
- Action potentials characterized by a plateau

Q: What is the typical duration of a spike potential in unitary smooth muscle?
A: Between 10 and 50 milliseconds.

Q: What external and internal factors can trigger a spike potential in smooth muscle?
A:
- Electrical stimulation
- Hormonal signals
- Neurotransmitter action
- Physical stretching of the muscle
- Spontaneous generation within the fiber

Q: Describe the appearance and duration of a plateau action potential in smooth muscle.
A: The initial phase is similar to a spike potential, but repolarization is significantly delayed, often lasting several hundred milliseconds to a full second (1000 ms).

Q: What is the functional purpose of a plateau during a smooth muscle action potential?
A: It facilitates the long-lasting muscle contractions required by specific organs, such as the ureter, the vascular system, and the uterus in certain states.

Q: Which ion channels are most responsible for generating the action potential in smooth muscle?
A: Voltage-gated calcium channels.

Q: Why is sodium's contribution to the smooth muscle action potential minimal?
A: Smooth muscle cell membranes possess a high density of voltage-gated calcium channels but have very few voltage-gated sodium channels.

Q: How do the kinetics of smooth muscle calcium channels compare to sodium channels in other excitable tissues?
A: Calcium channels open and close much more slowly than sodium channels.

Q: What are the two simultaneous roles performed by calcium ions entering the smooth muscle cell during an action potential?
A: They provide the electrical current needed for the action potential and directly initiate the muscle's contractile process.

Q: What are "slow waves" in the context of smooth muscle membrane potentials?
A: They are local, rhythmical fluctuations in membrane potential that are a self-excitatory property of some smooth muscle masses.

Q: Are slow waves considered action potentials?
A: No, they are local potentials that do not propagate themselves and do not directly trigger muscle contraction.

Q: Under what condition does a slow wave result in an action potential?
A: When the potential of the slow wave reaches the threshold of approximately -35 millivolts.

Q: What happens when the peak of a slow wave crosses the -35 millivolt threshold?
A: One or more action potentials are triggered, which then spread through the muscle and cause contraction.

Q: What is the alternative name for slow waves due to their ability to trigger rhythmic contractions?
A: Pacemaker waves.

Q: What are the two suggested causes for the rhythmic nature of slow waves?
A:
- Rhythmical variations in the conductance of ion channels
- The rising and falling activity of the pump that moves positive ions (like sodium) out of the cell

Q: How does physical stretch induce contraction in visceral smooth muscle?
A: Stretching decreases the negative charge of the membrane potential and combines with existing slow waves to spontaneously generate action potentials.

Q: What is the physiological importance of stretch-induced action potentials in the intestinal tract?
A: It allows the gut wall to automatically create peristaltic waves to move contents away when the intestine becomes overfilled.

Q: Why do multi-unit smooth muscle fibers rarely produce self-propagating action potentials?
A: The fibers are too small to generate the voltage change required to initiate a propagated action potential.

Q: What type of electrical change occurs in multi-unit smooth muscle when stimulated by nerves?
A: A junctional potential, which is a local depolarization.

Q: How does a junctional potential trigger contraction in a multi-unit smooth muscle fiber?
A: The depolarization spreads electrotonically over the entire small fiber, which is sufficient to cause contraction without a formal action potential.

Q: What are two factors that can trigger smooth muscle contraction without the presence of action potentials?
A:
- Various hormones
- Local tissue chemical factors

Q: How do hormones or local chemicals typically cause smooth muscle inhibition?
A: By closing sodium or calcium channels to prevent positive ion entry, or by opening potassium channels to allow positive ions to leave the cell.

Q: What is the term for the state of increased negativity inside a smooth muscle cell that prevents contraction?
A: Hyperpolarization.

Q: How can a hormone trigger smooth muscle contraction without changing the membrane potential?
A: By binding to a receptor that induces the internal release of calcium ions from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Q: Which second messengers are associated with the inhibition of smooth muscle contraction?
A: Cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) and cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP).

Q: How do second messengers like cAMP or cGMP reduce smooth muscle tension?
A: They activate pumps that move calcium ions into the sarcoplasmic reticulum or out of the cell, lowering the cytosolic calcium levels required for contraction.

Q: How do ions flow between unitary smooth muscle cells to ensure they contract as a single unit?
A: They travel through gap junctions that connect the cell membranes, allowing action potentials or ion flow to move from fiber to fiber.

Q: What is the "latent period" in smooth muscle contraction?
A: It is a 200 to 300 millisecond delay between a stimulus and the start of contraction, representing the time needed for calcium to diffuse from the extracellular fluid into the cell.

Q: What determines whether a hormone or neurotransmitter excites or inhibits a specific smooth muscle?
A: The type of receptor protein present on the muscle cell membrane (excitatory vs. inhibitory).

topics.topic: Explain the phenomena of stress relaxation and reverse stress relaxation in smooth muscles.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the physiological definition of the phenomenon known as stress-relaxation in smooth muscle?
A: It is the capacity of smooth muscle to return to nearly its baseline contractile force within seconds or minutes after it has been suddenly stretched or lengthened.

Q: In which specific variety of smooth muscle is the stress-relaxation phenomenon most commonly observed?
A: It is a characteristic primarily associated with visceral unitary smooth muscle, which is found in the walls of many hollow organs.

Q: What is the initial effect on a hollow organ's internal pressure when it undergoes a sudden increase in fluid volume?
A: The sudden stretch of the walls causes an immediate and substantial spike in internal pressure.

Q: Following an initial pressure spike due to stretching, how long does it typically take for the pressure to return to its original level in smooth muscle?
A: The pressure usually stabilizes back to its baseline level within about 15 to 60 seconds.

Q: Does the return to baseline pressure during stress-relaxation occur because the stretch is removed?
A: No, the pressure returns to its original level even while the stretch on the muscle wall is maintained.

Q: What happens if a hollow organ's volume is increased by a second step immediately after it has stabilized from a previous stretch?
A: The stress-relaxation process repeats, meaning the pressure will spike again and then return to the original level over the next minute.

Q: How is the phenomenon of reverse stress-relaxation defined?
A: It is the process where a sudden decrease in volume causes an immediate drop in pressure, followed by a recovery where the pressure rises back to the original level within a short period.

Q: What is the immediate reaction of a hollow organ's internal pressure when its volume is suddenly reduced?
A: The pressure initially experiences a drastic and sharp decline.

Q: What is the primary functional purpose of stress-relaxation and reverse stress-relaxation in the human body?
A: These mechanisms allow hollow organs to maintain a relatively consistent internal pressure despite significant and lasting changes in the volume of their contents.

Q: Using the urinary bladder as an example, describe the result of stress-relaxation.
A: When the bladder fills and its walls are stretched by urine, the pressure inside rises sharply at first but then drops back to its original state within a minute so that the organ can accommodate the new volume without a permanent pressure increase.

Q: How long do the effects of stress-relaxation or reverse stress-relaxation typically last regarding the stabilization of pressure?
A: The stabilization occurs over a period of seconds to several minutes, specifically mentioned as 15 to 60 seconds for the recovery phase in some instances.

topics.topic: Explain the LATCH mechanism
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: In smooth muscle, what is the primary purpose of the latch mechanism?
A: It allows the tissue to maintain a powerful, long-term contraction while consuming very little energy.

Q: How does the latch mechanism influence the level of stimulation needed to maintain a contraction?
A: It enables the muscle to keep its full contractile force even after the initial excitatory signal from nerves or hormones has been significantly lowered.

Q: How does the energy requirement for maintaining a contraction via the latch mechanism compare to skeletal muscle?
A: Smooth muscle can maintain a contraction using significantly less energy, sometimes as little as 1/300th of what skeletal muscle would require for a similar tension.

Q: What is the typical duration for which the latch mechanism can sustain tonic contractions?
A: It can facilitate these contractions for several hours.

Q: According to the postulated mechanism, what occurs when both myosin kinase and myosin phosphatase are highly active?
A: The contraction velocity is high and the myosin heads cycle through attachments to actin rapidly.

Q: How does the deactivation of myosin kinase and phosphatase contribute to the latch phenomenon?
A: It allows the myosin heads to stay bound to actin filaments for a much larger portion of their cycling time.

Q: Why is the consumption of ATP so low during the latch state of smooth muscle?
A: Energy is only used when a myosin head detaches from actin; in this state, heads remain attached for long periods, so ATP is rarely degraded.

Q: What determines the static force of contraction during the latch state?
A: The force is maintained by the high number of myosin heads that remain attached to the actin filaments at any specific moment.

Q: What is the effect of the latch mechanism on the cycling frequency of myosin heads?
A: The frequency at which the heads attach and detach is greatly reduced compared to normal contraction.

Q: Which two enzymes' activity levels are central to the regulation of the latch phenomenon?
A: - Myosin light chain kinase
- Myosin phosphatase

Q: What is the metabolic advantage of the latch mechanism for the body's internal organs?
A: It allows organs like the intestines and bladder to maintain tonic contractions almost indefinitely without exhausting the body's energy stores.

topics.topic: Describe the significance of LATCH mechanism.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: What is the primary purpose of the latch mechanism in smooth muscle?
A: This mechanism enables the muscle to hold a maximum force of contraction for long durations while utilizing very little energy.

Q: How does the excitation requirement change once smooth muscle has reached full contraction via the latch mechanism?
A: The necessary level of ongoing stimulation can be decreased significantly below the starting level while the muscle still maintains its total contractile force.

Q: How does the energy expenditure of the smooth muscle latch mechanism compare to sustained skeletal muscle activity?
A: The energy used to keep the muscle contracted can be as low as 1/300th of the energy that skeletal muscle would require for a similar sustained effort.

Q: What is the significance of the latch mechanism regarding the duration of smooth muscle activity?
A: It allows the muscle to maintain a state of tonic contraction for many hours.

Q: Does the latch mechanism require high levels of continuous external signaling?
A: No, very little ongoing input from hormonal pathways or nerve fibers is needed once the state is established.

Q: What molecular change occurs to the cycling frequency of myosin heads during the latch phenomenon?
A: The frequency of cycling decreases significantly as the activation of myosin kinase and myosin phosphatase enzymes drops.

Q: Why does smooth muscle maintain tension during the latch state despite a decrease in enzyme activation?
A: The myosin heads remain bound to actin for a much higher percentage of the total cycling time, keeping a large number of cross-bridges attached.

Q: What physical factor determines the static force of contraction during the latch state?
A: The force is determined by the specific number of myosin heads that are attached to the actin filaments at any given moment.

Q: Why is ATP consumption minimal during the latch mechanism?
A: ATP is only converted to ADP when a myosin head detaches from actin, which occurs very infrequently during this state.

Q: How does the deactivation of myosin kinase and phosphatase contribute to the "latching" effect?
A: Reducing the activity of these enzymes causes the myosin heads to stay attached to actin filaments for longer and longer intervals.

topics.topic: Explain the nervous and hormonal control of Smooth Muscle Contraction.
topics.generatedNotes:
Q: How does the stimulation of smooth muscle fibers differ from that of skeletal muscle fibers?
A: While skeletal muscle is only activated by the nervous system, smooth muscle can be triggered by nerve signals, hormonal influences, physical stretch, or changes in the chemical environment.

Q: What accounts for the diverse responses of smooth muscle to various stimuli?
A: The smooth muscle cell membrane contains various receptor proteins that can either start the contraction process or actively inhibit it.

Q: What is the typical structure of the nervous system's connection to smooth muscle?
A: Instead of the highly organized motor end plates found in skeletal muscle, autonomic nerves form diffuse junctions where they branch out and release neurotransmitters into the surrounding matrix.

Q: What are the bulbous enlargements found on the terminal axons of nerves supplying smooth muscle?
A: These are called varicosities, which are points where Schwann cells are interrupted to allow the release of transmitter substances.

Q: Which neurotransmitters are primarily released by the autonomic nerve endings in smooth muscle?
A: The vesicles in these nerve endings typically contain either acetylcholine or norepinephrine.

Q: How do neurotransmitters reach the muscle cells in a diffuse junction?
A: They are secreted into the interstitial matrix and must diffuse across a space that can range from a few nanometers to several micrometers.

Q: What are "contact junctions" in the context of smooth muscle innervation?
A: These are specialized junctions, common in multi-unit smooth muscle, where varicosities are separated from the muscle cell by only 20 to 30 nanometers, allowing for faster signaling.

Q: Why can the same neurotransmitter excite smooth muscle in one organ but inhibit it in another?
A: The response is determined by the specific receptor protein on the muscle cell surface; some receptors are excitatory while others are inhibitory.

Q: What is the typical relationship between acetylcholine and norepinephrine regarding smooth muscle action?
A: They generally produce opposite effects; if acetylcholine stimulates a specific muscle, norepinephrine will usually suppress it, and vice versa.

Q: What is the standard resting membrane potential for smooth muscle cells?
A: It usually ranges between -50 and -60 millivolts.

Q: Which ion is primarily responsible for generating action potentials in smooth muscle?
A: Calcium ions are the main participants, as smooth muscle membranes possess many voltage-gated calcium channels but very few voltage-gated sodium channels.

Q: Why are smooth muscle action potentials often characterized by a plateau?
A: Calcium channels open and close much more slowly than sodium channels, allowing for the prolonged depolarization seen in tissues like the uterus or ureter.

Q: What are "slow wave potentials" in visceral smooth muscle?
A: These are rhythmic, spontaneous fluctuations in the membrane potential that are not action potentials themselves but can trigger them if the threshold is reached.

Q: What is the threshold voltage required to elicit an action potential in most visceral smooth muscle?
A: Approximately -35 millivolts.

Q: How does physical stretch lead to the contraction of visceral smooth muscle?
A: Sufficient stretching generates spontaneous action potentials by combining with existing slow waves and decreasing the overall negativity of the membrane.

Q: How do multi-unit smooth muscles typically contract without an action potential?
A: The neurotransmitter causes a local depolarization known as a "junctional potential," which spreads throughout the small muscle fiber to initiate contraction.

Q: Approximately what percentage of smooth muscle contraction is estimated to be initiated without action potentials?
A: About 50 percent.

Q: Which local tissue factors can cause smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation in small blood vessels?
A: - A lack of oxygen
- Increased carbon dioxide levels
- Higher hydrogen ion concentration (acidity)
- Presence of adenosine or lactic acid
- Increased potassium ions
- Elevated body temperature
- Nitric oxide

Q: How does a drop in blood pressure lead to the dilation of small blood vessels?
A: Reduced pressure results in less physical stretch on the vascular smooth muscle, causing it to relax.

Q: List the major hormones that can influence smooth muscle contraction.
A: - Norepinephrine
- Epinephrine
- Angiotensin II
- Endothelin
- Vasopressin
- Oxytocin
- Serotonin
- Histamine

Q: By what mechanisms can a hormone inhibit smooth muscle contraction?
A: It can close calcium and sodium channels to prevent ion entry or open potassium channels to cause hyperpolarization (increased internal negativity).

Q: How can hormones trigger smooth muscle contraction without altering the membrane potential?
A: They can bind to receptors that trigger the internal release of calcium ions from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Q: Which intracellular second messengers are often involved in the hormonal inhibition of smooth muscle?
A: Cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) and cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP).

Q: How do cAMP and cGMP facilitate smooth muscle relaxation?
A: They activate enzymes that reduce sarcoplasmic calcium by pumping it out of the cell or back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Q: According to the classification of nerve fibers, what type of fibers supply internal organs like smooth muscle?
A: Visceral or autonomic nerve fibers.

Q: How are nerve fibers classified based on the type of neurotransmitter they secrete?
A: - Adrenergic fibers secrete noradrenalin
